title: Chapter 8 — Assessment in the Age of Claude
CHAPTER 8 —
ASSESSMENT IN THE AGE OF CLAUDE
segments.body_html:
Before the semester begins, before the syllabus is printed, before any student opens the assignment prompt — something h

Chapter 8 — Assessment in the Age of Cla — This is not a new kind of academic dishonesty. It is a new condition of the assignment.

beats.text: Before the semester begins, before the syllabus is printed, before any student opens the assignment prompt , something has already changed. The take-home essay that once required a student to sit alone with sources, struggle with an argument, and write sentences they had not seen before can now be drafted in ninety seconds with a prompt to Claude. This is not a new kind of academic dishonesty. It is a new condition of the assignment.

segments.label: 01 / 13 · HOOK

segments.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO
title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS
YOU DO.

·Identify which parts of your current assessments are vulnerable to AI substitution and which parts are not
·Apply five assessment design patterns that produce evidence of student thinking
·Redesign a vulnerable take-home assessment into a process-based design
·Write an AI-use disclosure requirement that is teachable, not punitive
·Distinguish between assessment redesign and surveillance, and understand why the distinction matters

beats.text: By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: Identify which parts of your current assessments are vulnerable to AI substitution and which parts are not Apply five assessment design patterns that produce evidence of student thinking Redesign a vulnerable take-home assessment into a process-based design Write an AI-use disclosure requirement that is teachable, not punitive Distinguish between assessment redesign and surveillance, and understand why the distinction matters

segments.label: 02 / 13 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

title: CORE CONCEPTS
segments.body_html: Assessment validity→Authentic assessment→Process evidence→AI-use disclosure→Cognitive labor

beats.text: Assessment validity means the assessment measures what it claims to measure. An essay prompt that claims to assess a student's ability to construct an argument is not valid if the evidence of argument-construction has been produced by Claude. Wiggins and McTighe's work on backward design puts validity at the center of assessment: start from the evidence you need, then design the task that produces it (Wiggins and McTighe, Understanding by Design). Generative AI has not changed this principle; it has made violations of it more consequential.

segments.label: 03 / 13 · CORE CONCEPTS

title: THE VULNERABILITY
MAP.
segments.body_html:
·Take-home essays on general or well-documented topics
·Research summaries
·Multiple-choice and short-answer tests given as take-home or untimed work
·Discussion board posts on abstract topics
·Lab or case reports with standard templates

beats.text: Not all assessments are equally vulnerable to AI substitution. A rough map: High vulnerability: Take-home essays on general or well-documented topics Research summaries Multiple-choice and short-answer tests given as take-home or untimed work Discussion board posts on abstract topics Lab or case reports with standard templates

segments.label: 04 / 13 · THE VULNERABILITY MAP

title: FIVE ASSESSMENT
DESIGN PATTERNS.
segments.body_html:
·A pre-writing brainstorm or outline showing the student's thinking before drafting
·Two or more drafts with revision notes explaining what the student changed and why
A brief reflectionWhat was hard? What did you learn from revising? What would you change if you had more time?

beats.text: Add a live conversation to any written assessment. After students submit work, meet with them , individually or in small groups , for a five-to-ten-minute conversation about what they wrote. Ask them to explain a claim, walk through their reasoning, identify a counterargument they did not address, or connect their argument to a new case you introduce in the conversation. A student who engaged seriously with the written work can answer these questions. A student whose written work was produced by Claude without engagement will have difficulty.

segments.label: 05 / 13 · FIVE ASSESSMENT DESIGN PATTERN

Transfer elementThe essay prompt is refined to include a case the teacher introduces the week before the due date — a specific recent ev
Oral checkIn the week after submission, the teacher holds brief (five-minute) check-ins with students during office hours. One que

beats.text: A university sociology course assigns a five-page take-home essay: Analyze how social media platforms affect adolescent identity formation. Use at least five peer-reviewed sources. Due in two weeks.

segments.label: 06 / 13 · REDESIGNING A VULNERABLE ASSES

title: ON DETECTION AND
SURVEILLANCE
segments.body_html:
This chapter does not recommend AI detection tools as a primary response to generative AI. The reasons are practical and

On Detection and Surveillance — Practically: current AI detection tools produce false positives — they flag authentic student work as AI-generated — at

beats.text: This chapter does not recommend AI detection tools as a primary response to generative AI. The reasons are practical and principled. Practically: current AI detection tools produce false positives , they flag authentic student work as AI-generated , at rates that are unacceptable for high-stakes decisions (U.S. Department of Education AI report). The tools are also easy to circumvent and will remain so as AI writing becomes more varied. Detection-based responses build an adversarial relationship between teacher and student without solving the underlying assessment design problem.

segments.label: 07 / 13 · ON DETECTION AND SURVEILLANCE

title: A NOTE
ON EQUITY.
segments.body_html:
Surveillance-heavy assessment responses to AI — mandatory keystroke logging, continuous camera monitoring, algorithmic f

A Note on Equity — Assessment redesign distributes burden more equitably: everyone does the process work, everyone participates in the oral

beats.text: Surveillance-heavy assessment responses to AI , mandatory keystroke logging, continuous camera monitoring, algorithmic flagging , impose burdens unevenly. Students who lack reliable internet access, students with test anxiety, students who are first-generation and already operating under high scrutiny, and students with disabilities are disproportionately affected by monitoring regimes. The burden lands on students who are often already at the margins, not on the AI systems that prompted the response.

segments.label: 08 / 13 · A NOTE ON EQUITY

title: THE HUMAN
GATE.
segments.body_html:
·What learning is this assessment measuring? State it in one sentence. If you cannot, the assessment is not ready.
·What can Claude produce that would pass this assessment without the student understanding the material? Be honest. This
·What design feature creates evidence of student thinking? If the answer is "the student promised not to use AI," that is
Is the AI-use boundary teachable? Students need to understand clearly what is permitted, what requires disclosure, and wand those rules need to be consistent with the course's learning goals.
·Have equity implications been considered? Does this design penalize students with unreliable access to technology, stude

beats.text: Before implementing any assessment change, the teacher review questions: What learning is this assessment measuring? State it in one sentence. If you cannot, the assessment is not ready.

segments.label: 09 / 13 · THE HUMAN GATE

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Reacting to AI with prohibition only.→Ignoring the equity dimension of surveillance.→Redesigning so much that students stop learning from assessment.→Treating all AI use as equivalent.→Waiting for institutional policy before acting.

beats.text: Reacting to AI with prohibition only. Saying "no AI tools" is a policy statement, not an assessment design. It may be the right policy for some assessments, but it does not change what the assessment measures or how it produces evidence. Ignoring the equity dimension of surveillance. AI detection tools and monitoring software are not neutral. Before implementing them, ask who bears the burden and whether the assessment design could instead produce the evidence without surveillance.

segments.label: 10 / 13 · COMMON MISTAKES

title: BRIDGE
segments.body_html:
This chapter closes the book's Act Two. Chapters 2 through 8 have built teacher-facing workflows: from learning outcomes

Bridge — The teacher is still the system. Act Three asks what the system needs to hold.

beats.text: This chapter closes the book's Act Two. Chapters 2 through 8 have built teacher-facing workflows: from learning outcomes through lesson planning, feedback, differentiation, rubrics, and now assessment redesign. Chapter 9 begins Act Three: the classroom and institutional questions that require going beyond the teacher's workflow to the conditions under which teaching happens , privacy, student data, institutional policy, and the terms on which students engage with AI. The teacher is still the system. Act Three asks what the system needs to hold.
